windows.text: The March of Time. News of life. A magazine which tells the news of the world and the ways of its people in words and pictures. 16 million American men, women and children, nine readers per copy, stirring to the strangely compelling desire to see, to know, to be informed, are now reading life each week. And as life goes into its second year, US business is discovering what the US reading public has already discovered. The life's force, the impact of pictures which attract and inform and impel.

windows.text: in the person of this man named Miller. To begin with, it isn't simply a struggle of the state to get the church, Protestant and Catholic, out of politics. If it had been, I think there would have been much sympathy for the state. That would have been a conflict like the one over the separation of churches state in France, the generation of gold. And people in this country, I think, would have felt that the church had better confined itself to spiritual life and leave politics alone. But that wasn't the issue. A church out of politics can be just as free as one in politics, perhaps even more so. But the Nazis didn't want a free church. They wanted to do a brute Christianity in Germany. They disliked its Hebrew origin.

windows.text: the cloister. He wasn't unmodern without sympathy for the Hitler revolution. He wasn't a pale aesthetic. He was a fighter. During the World War, he was commander of a submarine, and for distinguished bravery won the highest German decoration, the Holy Marie. He loved the daring and the excitement of war, and he was fervently patriotic. And when peace came, rather than sink his submarine at Scappa's flow at the behest of the British, he resigned and became a farm worker, hoping to save up money enough to own his own land. Then German inflation wiped out his savings, and he entered the church, working his way through theological seminary as a day laborer.

windows.text: I had occasion to speak of British foreign policy. The big headline during that week was Anthony Eden resigns as foreign secretary, and when we reflect that that was less than 8 months ago, we see how fast and how furiously history is being made today. Britain's goods for future crises is a typical headline these days. The resignation of Eden marked the end of the League of Nations system of collective security in Europe, and the beginning of what may be called the Chamberlain system of direct negotiation, which was to mean sitting down to talk Turkey to the dictators instead of making faces at them from behind a barrage of high principles. This system eventually led quite logically to the conferences of Bechtisgaden, Goldisberg, and Unik.

Just to the right is the tidal basin around which just last week the cherry blossoms were in full bloom. But now a word concerning Miss Marion Anderson. Marion Anderson's rise to worldwide fame is quite typically an American success story. windows.text: I arrived on August 14th on the Queen Mary to travel in England, Scotland and France, and at the last minute book to return passage on the Athenia. I was in the third class dining room on Sunday night. I had been dreadfully sick all day, and had just finished a very light supper when the man next to me said, I think I'll set my watch. I was just in the process of setting my own watch when the loud crash of the explosion came, and the support in the dining room, which was practically beside me, came crashing down. Dishes went flying all over the place, lights were extinguished immediately. The boat lurched to the port side and everyone went hurtling across the floor.
windows.index_text: Survivors Recall the Sinking of the SS Athenia. BBC, 1939-09-06. I arrived on August 14th on the Queen Mary to travel in England, Scotland and France, and at the last minute book to return passage on the Athenia. I was in the third class dining room on Sunday night. I had been dreadfully sick all day, and had just finished a very light supper when the man next to me said, I think I'll set my watch. I was just in the process of setting my own watch when the loud crash of the explosion came, and the support in the dining room, which was practically beside me, came crashing down. Dishes went flying all over the place, lights were extinguished immediately. The boat lurched to the port side and everyone went hurtling across the floor.

windows.text: The fumes were so thick that we thought we would be asphyxiated, and the smell was something terrible. Everyone was crying, oh my god, and we never really expected to see daylight. Somehow, I will never know how, I reached the stairs, being separated from my friends in the meantime. When I got up on deck on the port side, I should say about a mile away, I saw a column of smoke which I took to be from the submarine. There were many dead lying around, and it wasn't very pleasant of course. I couldn't get down into my cabin to get a life-preserver, and dashed frantically around asking everyone where I might find one. They suggested I go into any of the cabins, but I wasn't familiar with the first-class cabins which were nearest to the life-belts, and consequently hardly knew which way to turn.

When I got up on deck on the port side, I should say about a mile away, I saw a column of smoke which I took to be from the submarine. There were many dead lying around, and it wasn't very pleasant of course. I couldn't get down into my cabin to get a life-preserver, and dashed frantically around asking everyone where I might find one. They suggested I go into any of the cabins, but I wasn't familiar with the first-class cabins which were nearest to the life-belts, and consequently hardly knew which way to turn.

windows.text: Someone finally threw me a life-belt, and a man helped me put it on. A rope ladder was thrown overboard, and I was just about six feet from the water, when I noticed that the ladder was tangled at the bottom, and I could not possibly proceed any further. The man above was practically on top of my hands all the time, and I was wondering how much longer I could hang on, dangling in the air, when a man who was in the lifeboat flung me a rope which I managed to slide down. There were thirty or thirty-five people in the boat, and we were just ready to push off when we saw a woman struggling in the water. We finally got her into the boat, although it was found that she had broken her arm. As we were pushing off, we saw several women coming down a rope ladder, but they slipped right into the water, and I suppose were dashed against the side of the boat.

windows.text: I never saw them after that. A little later I heard another explosion, and it seemed as if it were right under the lifeboat. It seemed a lifetime until we were finally picked up. I was so exhausted and weak that I had to be helped down to the boat and just flop down and had to make the best of things. I was asleep when the explosion came. The terrific force pitched me down on the floor. Immediately there was a complete black-hot. I couldn't see to collect anything, not even my life jacket. I distinctly remember hearing two detonations almost simultaneously. I found my way out of my cabin and started the struggle to get on deck. I have in mind a general impression of wrecked and distorted steelwork in splintered wood, an awful sound of rushing water and almost overpowering flames.

windows.text: As I made my way to the deck, I encountered at many points the bodies of those who must have been killed in the explosion. When I reached the deck, the scene was remarkably ordered, everything considered. Those who could made their way to their boat stations, but gradually as the boats were slow and being cleared, there was a series of rushes between boat stations. Women and children were being flung into any boat which was ready to take off. Out to sea, I just think we saw puffs of smoke from shells as if the submarine had been firing in an attempt to get the wireless. I helped to get the passengers into the lifeboat at my own station and also into one nearby They were quickly filled, and with a chef who had been assisting me, we made our way to the other side of the ship. windows.text: a boat which was being lowered into the water until to jump. We managed to get away fairly safely. There were, however, few people on board who knew much about seamanship, and passengers and crew alike, took a hand in the rowing. Presently, a launch came alongside, and an officer came on board and sorted things out. Later the lights of the Athenia came on, and it was then more easy to see our whereabouts. By this time we managed to find flares, and it settled the women and children as comfortably as possible in the bottom of the boat. Altogether we were aboard the lifeboat for about ten hours before being taken on one of the rescue ships at 6.15 in the morning. back on it now, it seems one of the quickest nights I have spent, and is left in my mind and a vague mixture of nightmare and stark reality.

windows.text: A warning telegram from the Ministry of Health set the thing in motion. We immediately took steps to restricted missions here to acute cases and to evacuate all patients who were fit to be moved. Sixty students had been trained to act to stretch a batter as to carry the patients and the wards to the waiting ambulances. There were 600 patients in the hospital, and in a few days more than half of these had been taken to their homes by their relatives or by private individuals who volunteered to lend their cars. A hundred and fifty who still required hospital treatment were sent by buses converted into ambulances and then by train to provincial hospital. Our patients were evacuated to every part of the country without a hitch

windows.text: Since the outbreak of war, our large outpatients department have been transferred to the basement of a new steel and concrete building, which has been further protected with some 100,000 sandbags filled and placed in position by the medical students and the permanent staff. In our new quarters, we are equipped as efficiently as in peacetime for the treatment of patients. The wards, X-ray department, operating theaters, and other special departments are near at hand. The space available may be rather smaller, but it is much safer and furthermore, it is so arranged as to enable us quickly to dispose and treat any sudden rush of raid casualties.

Nothing will be really important now until he jumps from his chair under the arc lights when the bell sounds for the first round. Meanwhile, he must spend the day going through the motions of eating, sleeping, and diverting himself, but always he knows that life will again seem important to him, only when the second step out of the ring can he stand spacing his opponent with his whole future at stake. For the people of Britain, life on the second plane consisted looking forward to the coming operations, wondering about them speculating, hoping confident that when the attack begins daily life will again take on significance in me. I think it's partly from this feeling of suspended existence that it's been responsible for the wave of strikes in Britain.

Meanwhile, he must spend the day going through the motions of eating, sleeping, and diverting himself, but always he knows that life will again seem important to him, only when the second step out of the ring can he stand spacing his opponent with his whole future at stake. For the people of Britain, life on the second plane consisted looking forward to the coming operations, wondering about them speculating, hoping confident that when the attack begins daily life will again take on significance in me. I think it's partly from this feeling of suspended existence that it's been responsible for the wave of strikes in Britain.

windows.text: Well, I've been struck by the contrast between the very old and the very new tradition existing beside advanced economic and sociological thinking, final buildings beside modern architecture. The thing that struck me first and the hardest is the general feeling of calm confidence. It wasn't that way, in 39. It wasn't that way at all. War had just been declared. No one knew what was going to happen in any next minute. But they were quite ready to believe it would be something awful. Children were being evacuated from the big cities. Everyone was carrying gas masks at all times. Even waiters in restaurants wore them, strapped on behind like a bustle while waiting on tables. Well, it isn't like that now.

windows.text: After all, this home front has been a very active war front for a long time. You mean people don't have to wonder about what sort of things may happen? That's it, John. They've been through everything that can happen to a home front and they know all the answers. So, they just go on about their business, calmly, confidently, completely unafraid. To me, it's, well, it's more than reassuring. It's really inspiring. Do you think this British attitude has had its effect on all our people here? I think it must have had. Competence and calm are just as contagious, just as catching as our hysteria, bewilderment and the jitters of any kind. Certainly even the little time I've been here has been long enough to show very plainly

windows.text: that our folks here aren't suffering from any kind of jitters. And I think the British attitudes must have been a big help to them when they were getting settled in. I've been noticing a lot of little things. For instance, a notice in the papers, proposing a share of the ride scheme for taxi cabs. That's a little amazing, you know, because we've been doing that in many American cities for a long time. And I think rationing is much more complete here than at home. The ordinary man doesn't seem to be as affected by inflation as his counterpart in the States. As long as he keeps living an ordinary life, he's all right. It's when he wants to kick up his heel, but he feels the pinch. But even the ordinary man has to pay 50 cents a pack for his cigarettes, George.

windows.text: And I think you'll get a shark when you go to the movies and have to fork over a couple of dollars a seat. But Bill, were conditions here any different from what you expected? Yes, I worked on. I'd expect it to see things generally. It seems a lot more than they are. I thought the people would be changed more by what they've gone through. I thought all the physical hardships. the law of taxation, the rationing, all that sort of thing. I thought it would show more on the surface. Actually, it hardly shows at all. On the surface. What's your first impression of the American army over here, Bill? Well, of course, I really haven't had a chance to see much of it. Nothing of it in the field. But the judge, just from what you can see in London, I'd say that our army here is an army ready to go. Certainly the morale is tops.

windows.text: And I'm told that a degree, the perfection of training, is just as high. You came over with a Navy George. What's that angle? Well, I got a wonderful tick out of the way men who are once bankers and butchers and beer salesmen have pitched in with the regulars. As an American, it made me pretty proud to see the way they're doing a terrifically complicated business of running convoys and fighting ships. Any incidents you remember in particular? Well, I can't say much, of course, but there was one story that I think is worth telling. It was the story of a colored mess attendant. He used to spend all his spare time studying up on plain recognition, silhouettes and that sort of thing. One of the officers heard about him and decided to give him a test.

This colored mess attendant quietly identified 100 out of 100. They set the machine to 100th of a second per flash. The mess attendant's average fell to 95 out of 100. Then half apologetically, he proceeded to give the nationalities and general types of the remaining five planes. And now when the bosons whistle sounds all hands to general quarters, that slight shy steward's mate is not a mess attendant anymore. He's assistant to the gunnery officer for the whole ship pretty good. He's the lucky man to have eyes like that Speaking of ships. What do you hope to do covering the Navy in the invasion George?

But certainly I can say that I came by air in one of the big planes of our air transport command, and that it was a very nice trip, not so much as a bump all the way from Washington to Wells and to the place we landed somewhere in the United Kingdom.

windows.text: Why touch your cheek? Is it not hot and tight and dry? And look what light climbs up the eastern sky and sinks and climbs again. Like to the brighter aurora of the north, it floods and flashes, pulses, pales. Then glows, lighting the entire east majestically, as if it were the sun that rose. I wish it were. Surely our fiber and sinew, the backbone and brain of us, are made of sunless, common stuff than clay. Surely the blood which warms the veins of heroes at the front, our brothers and our sons, runs also in our own.

windows.text: And are we not then capable, perhaps, of something more courageous than we yet have shown. Surely some talisman, some token of our love to pride in them, our heavy gratitude and so much so much love will find its way to them. Some messenger, the vicar and the angels of what we feel, will fly before them where they fly, before them and above. Like patron goddesses in wars of old, cleaving with level, lovely brows, the hard air before the eager prowls, lighting their way within contestant wings and winged heel. This is the hour. This is the appointed time.

Of immediate events they expect these. Another big German attack north of the salient along the Maas in Roar. A Russian offensive into East Prussia. One of my men took a pole and shut it down 14 feet through the mud and sand. This meant that during the hell and fury of the landing, we had to throw away our plans, figured out something new,

windows.text: and pitch in with a lot of work. You mentioned the hell and fury of D-Day. What does a beachhead look like during those early days? Like something out of dandies and fernos. Shells are crashing. Sometimes the beaches are swept by rifle, mortar, and machine gun fire. The planes are fighting overhead with the japs doing their best to bomb our positions. On one beach where I was, 50 landing craft and 20 transports were all sure trying to unload. Supplies were all over the place. The rain was coming down in sheets and the ground was a sea of mud. Deep sticky mud. There weren't any such thing as roads. As usual, in the early hours of an invasion, there weren't enough men or trucks to move the supplies as quickly as you'd like.

windows.text: And in addition, on this beach, there were Filipinos wandering around some of them wounded. By the way, what was the reaction of the Filipino people? Well, it was wonderful. A lot different than some of us had expected. We'd been worried for fear that bringing the war to their homes and causing a lot of damage would create resentment, but not a bit of it. Whenever the Filipinos saw an American, they would repeat over and over again like a rhyme. We are very glad you are here. And they were. You could tell it from the way they smiled and looked. Their spirit and courage, an obvious joy at seeing us, gave the war a little added meaning. Commander, why is it the CDs that don't such a remarkable job wherever they've been assigned? The thing is, they are all skilled men.

This war must be waged, it is being waged, for the greatest and most persistent intensity. Everything we are, everything we have is at stake. Everything we are and have will be given. We have no question of the ultimate victory. We have no question of the cost.

windows.text: Our losses will be heavy, but we and our lives will go on fighting together to ultimate total victory. We have seen a year marked on the whole by substantial progress thought victory, even though the year ended with a setback for our arms. When the Germans launched a ferocious covert attack into Luxembourg and Belgium, with the obvious objectives of cutting our line in the center, our men are fought with indescribable and unforgettable gallantry under most difficult conditions. windows.text: As we continue the decisive phases of this war, the munitions that we expend will amount every day. I shall not go into the details of all production and the requirements of all materials. They are contained in the message that I sent today, and I hope that many of you will have an opportunity to read that in full. But there is one very human need that I do want to mention. We need 20,000 more trained nurses for our Army and Navy. Those nurses that we have are rendering gallant service to our sick and wounded men, but they have been called upon to do more than their share. More than a thousand nurses are now hospitalized

Those nurses that we have are rendering gallant service to our sick and wounded men, but they have been called upon to do more than their share. More than a thousand nurses are now hospitalized

windows.text: themselves, and part of this is due to overwork. At Army hospitals in the United States, there There is only one nurse to 26 beds instead of one to 15 beds as there should be. Since volunteering has not produced the number of nurses required, I ask the Congress and my message to amend the Selective Service Act to provide for the induction of registered nurses into the armed forces. The need is too pressing to await the outcome of further efforts of improving. However I urge registered nurses throughout the country, and there are several hundred thousand of them to volunteer immediately for this great service.

windows.text: for the prosecution of the war. I urge that this be done at the earliest possible moment. It is not too late in the war. In fact, bitter experience has shown that in this kind of mechanized warfare, where new weapons are constantly being created by our enemies and by ourselves, closer we come to the end of the war, the more pressing becomes the need for sustained war production, with which to deliver the final blow to the enemy. There are three basic arguments for a national service law. First, it would assure that we have the right numbers of workers in the right places at the right times.

windows.text: Second, it would provide supreme proof to all our fighting men that we are giving them what they're entitled to, which is nothing less than our total effort back home. And third, it would be the final unequivocal answer to the hopes of the Nazis and the Japanese, that we may become half-hearted about this war. And as they can get from us a negotiated peace, national service legislation would be used only to the extent absolutely required by military necessities. In fact, experience in Great Britain and in other nations at war indicates that use of the compulsory powers of national service is necessary only in very rare instances.

windows.text: In the field of foreign policy we propose to stand together with the United Nations, not of the war alone, but for the victory for which the war is fought. There is not only a common danger that unites us, but a common hope. Our is an association, not of governments, but of peoples. And the peoples hope is peace, here as in England, in England as in Russia, in Russia as in China, in France, through the continent of Europe and throughout the world, wherever men love freedom, the hope and purpose of the people offer peace, a peace that is durable and secure.

windows.text: Now it will not be easy to create this people's peace. We have seen already in areas liberated from the Nazi and the fascist tyranny what problems peace will bring. When we dilute ourselves, if we attempt to believe wishfully, that all these problems can be solved overnight, the firm foundation can be built, and it will be built. But the continuance and assurance of a living peace must in the long run be the work of the people themselves. We ourselves, like all peoples who have gone through the difficult processes of liberation and adjustment, know of our own experience how great the difficulties can be.

windows.text: are doing without essential supplies such as paper and textbooks, and in many cases are being taught by poorly trained substitute teachers. Many of the best teachers in our school systems have been so overworked and underpaid that they've left their profession and their places are filled with inadequate teachers or not filled at all. And the reason for all this? You, as a parent and a taxpayer, simply haven't been paying enough attention to what's been going on. The situation can be corrected. If you will investigate the educational conditions in your community, and join with community groups to take positive action that needs to be taken, if our children are not to be cheated out of the good education they deserve. Now once again, back to the NBC Newsroom.

windows.text: There, Salem, Oregon, a logging truck crashed through a bridge along a country road, completely wrecking it. And then officials learned that the stork was expected in three homes separated from hospitals and doctors by the missing bridge. So the county borrowed a movable bridge from the state hand is making an all-out effort to get it installed, and unless the stork shows an awful burst of speed they figure they'll make it. In Philadelphia, the city needed new motor cars for official work and advertised for bids with the old city cars offered as trade-ins. They received two offers from dealers who said they would supply new cars and pay additional cash as well. So Philadelphia is buying 258 new motorcars, trading in old ones and making $10,000 cash on the transaction, because the dealers can resell the old trade-ins for such a high price.

windows.text: Here's a letter from Mr. Nelson H. Watson, Brampton, Ontario, rural route number five. Does the atom bomb affect the weather? Can the experiments with uranium at chalk, river, porthole, etc. have anything to do with the extremes of weather? Well, Gil, what do you say about the atomic bomb and weather? Not much more, Bill, and I said last summer over the CBC in response to a similar question. Were it not for the risk of leaving a dangerous impression, I would say that the force of the atomic bomb has been overestimated. However, as a scientist, I hasten to add that the horrible effects of our experiments with the atomic bomb to date cannot be overestimated. Whatever I have to say about the relative energy of the bomb or the size of the explosion, I cannot caution people too strongly to remember the devastating result of the bomb measured in terms of human lives in destruction.

windows.text: It's difficult for anyone to comprehend the insignificance of an atomic bomb explosion compared with the energy of the Earth's atmosphere. By limiting the comparison to a single storm such as moves across Eastern Canada about every third day during the winter, we can get a better idea of the relative energy involved. It has been reliably calculated that the energy of the atomic bomb is approximately one-100,000th of the energy in a normal promised wide storm. In other words, it would take 100,000 atomic bombs to equal the energy of an average storm. To appreciate that figure, this difference is equivalent to the difference between the energy you would expend in lifting a piano and in lifting a feather. Perhaps a comparison of the area of the atomic bomb explosion with the area of a normal storm
windows.index_text: The Atom Bomb. CBC, 1948-04-03. It's difficult for anyone to comprehend the insignificance of an atomic bomb explosion compared with the energy of the Earth's atmosphere. By limiting the comparison to a single storm such as moves across Eastern Canada about every third day during the winter, we can get a better idea of the relative energy involved. It has been reliably calculated that the energy of the atomic bomb is approximately one-100,000th of the energy in a normal promised wide storm. In other words, it would take 100,000 atomic bombs to equal the energy of an average storm. To appreciate that figure, this difference is equivalent to the difference between the energy you would expend in lifting a piano and in lifting a feather. Perhaps a comparison of the area of the atomic bomb explosion with the area of a normal storm

windows.text: would give a better idea of why the bomb has had a negligible effect on the world's weather. Think of an average storm as about 1,500 miles in diameter and quite flat, very much like a photograph record. Now, to show the atomic bomb explosion on the same scale, you would have a spec on the photograph record too small to be seen without the aid of a magnifying glass. Let me repeat, I'm not minimizing the destruction and death which a company such man made explosions as the atomic bomb. I am saying that the magnitude of nature's forces cannot easily be grasped. In short, that the atomic bomb has had no noticeable effect on the world's weather.

windows.text: CBS News presents report from overseas. Today by transcription from London, Stephen Laird reports the average British view on local and foreign affairs. Now and then he suggests the British can cast aside their worries and concentrate on less pressing matters, and here now is Stephen Laird. Almost everybody in Britain today is supremely concentrated on one story in the news. A typical headline over this story says, England's still there and fighting back. You might suppose the British were at war somewhere in this troubled world, but no, they're simply doing better now in their international cricket match with the Australians. The British military is in all-out operation against rebellious natives in Malaya.

windows.text: The Palestine fighting is on again. The crisis continues in Berlin, but foreign news doesn't interest the British man and woman in the neighborhood the way local events do. The other week when the international tension began to mount the London stock market surprisingly held fairly even. At that time the trade paper financial times said that the market should because of domestic and international strains be taking a tumble. But fortunately said the financial times most British investors and speculators were entirely preoccupied with the annual ask-out horse race meeting. Just like you, the average man and woman in Britain, is concerned not so much about Palestine and Berlin, as they are about the cost of living. How to make ends meet?

windows.text: Next, there was agreement that supplying Berlin by air will not over any extended period of time be enough. The Russians must restore full road, rail and water communication between the western zones and Berlin. But then the disagreement began. The Americans proposed the stiff three-power protest direct to Moscow. The French didn't think too much of this. One of the Russians ignored the protest, the French asked. What are you Americans prepared to do about it? We ask, said the French, because it would humiliate and weaken the Western position in Europe if we have no effective means of backing up a strongly worded ultimatum to the Russian. The Americans replied that the United States was willing to make an unconditional stand in Berlin. The French said, good, but where are your troops?

windows.text: The board action was taken following a hearing and a petition for the creation of a township submitted by Custer Residence. Custer has been in the Warden township, which Custer Residence believed too large to adequately service the town of Custer. The board reported no protests were registered against the petition. Emerson Body, famous Negro minister and lecturer, spoke this morning before the student body of Eastern Montana College of Education on human and race relations. Dr. Body emphasized the fact that in world terms, the white race is in the minority, and that the shrinking of the world through improved methods of communication and transportation has forced man's attention to the necessity of believing in the theory of world brotherhood. Dr. Body will speak before the public tonight at the College Auditorium at 8 p.m.

A report out of France says that the foreign ministers of the big three nations, Britain, France and the United States will meet in London next month. The report comes from one of the ministers, French foreign minister Robert Truman. He says the big three diplomats will discuss a possible meeting with Russia and sources that the French foreign office say that the date of the meeting has been set for April the 12th, the same date foreign ministers of the 12 Atlantic PAC nations meet for discussion of mutual defense. In Washington, however, the State Department says that no date has been set for the big three meeting. The French premier's announcement said that the big three also will discuss the Far East, including Japan, relations with communist China, aid to the Southeast Asia and Germany.

windows.text: When King George opened the British Parliament today, he made no mention of one of the most talked about subjects in the British Isles. And that's the nationalization of the steel and the iron industries. Now Prime Minister Atley says we should not take that omission as a sign of the labor governments backing down. Atley said that the laborites have not the slightest intention of dropping the nationalization of steel in iron. Said he, the statute is on the statute books and our purposes to give effect to the acts passed by parliament. The nationalization law is slated to take effect the first of October with the actual take-over due in January. The Atley Statement was a challenge to Winston Churchill's conservatives to fight off the issue of nationalization in spite of a labor party's shaky majority in parliament.

windows.text: What are French cadets doing at Randolph? They're the first contingent of the some 1,300 foreign military personnel who will be training in this country by the end of June under the Mutual Defense Assistance Program. that program designed to bolster the defenses of the North Atlantic Treaty nations. There are 75 of these cadets, bright-eyed, eager, and fresh from their beloved France for the first time, almost none of them speaking any but their own tongue. Now this posed quite a problem for our instructors at Randall, almost none of whom spoke any but English, all of which led to some very fast French classes for the majors, captains and lieutenants of the faculty staff.
windows.index_text: Air Force Hour Triumph of Old Glory. AFRTS, 1950-06-03. What are French cadets doing at Randolph? They're the first contingent of the some 1,300 foreign military personnel who will be training in this country by the end of June under the Mutual Defense Assistance Program. that program designed to bolster the defenses of the North Atlantic Treaty nations. There are 75 of these cadets, bright-eyed, eager, and fresh from their beloved France for the first time, almost none of them speaking any but their own tongue. Now this posed quite a problem for our instructors at Randall, almost none of whom spoke any but English, all of which led to some very fast French classes for the majors, captains and lieutenants of the faculty staff.

windows.text: To their own surprise, by the time their young French charges arrived a month ago, the instructors were ready. Now Americans being nomads at heart are pretty much at home after a while almost anywhere they go, but a Frenchman out of France, and especially a young Frenchman. In our country, he finds many things strange to him. Some not so easy to become accustomed to. For example, their first meal at Randolph was quite a shock to the ruddy cheek young galeaks. No wine. The equivalent to an American would be no coffee at his table. However, they eventually became used to, and finally very farmed up, an excellent substitute.

windows.text: The GI haircut, to which all cadets must submit, also caused considerable concern among these guests from France, where the closer the hair is crap, well, where they just don't like short haircuts. And by this past midweek, Colonel John G. Fowler, commanding officer of the base, was able to report. In spite of language difficulties, the integration of the French students into the basic pilot training program here at Randolph Air Force Base is progressing rapidly and smoothly.

windows.text: Meanwhile, full-scale air war appears to be shaping up over Korea with the Reds for the fifth straight day, making determined efforts to break up the Allied raids, which were concentrated today on communist supply lines. Our Air Force claims nine locomotives destroyed, 19 others damaged, and one red-plained damage. The Reds again sent up more than 80 jet fighters to challenge our fighter bombers. In Washington, the critical talks with Iranian Premier Mosadek, over the British Iranian oil dispute, shifted today from the Capitol to a far-mouted nearby Middleburg, Virginia, As Assistant Secretary of State George McGee entertained the visiting premier, McGee took over the discussions since Secretary Atchison and the Party of Delegates to the UN Assembly were leaving today for Paris.

And tonight, you'll hear the choices of Matthew Houghton, Peter Sturzberg, Douglas Shantz and James M. Menophe. Senior in years of service is Matthew Halt and in London, whose assignments began in the most exciting days of the war. Now here is his account of May the 8th, 1945. windows.text: Berlin on B.E. Day. A day earlier, General Eisenhower had taken the unconditional surrender of the German armies at his headquarters in France. The next day, Air Marshall Tedder, who was Eisenhower's British deputy, flew into Berlin to join the Russian commander, Marshall Zhukov, in a similar ceremony. He could take with him only ten of the hundreds of war correspondents, and I had to be one of them. It was a lovely day, and the heart of the world seemed to be soaring with joy. We flew over the skeleton of Cologne Cathedral, and over the Rhine. At the Elbe, Russian fighters met us and escorted us into Berlin. And we flew into a vast cloud of smoke and dust. The great city of five million people was still smoking and burning.
windows.index_text: Foreign Correspondents Recall Their Best Stories. CBC, 1956-01-26. Berlin on B.E. Day. A day earlier, General Eisenhower had taken the unconditional surrender of the German armies at his headquarters in France. The next day, Air Marshall Tedder, who was Eisenhower's British deputy, flew into Berlin to join the Russian commander, Marshall Zhukov, in a similar ceremony. He could take with him only ten of the hundreds of war correspondents, and I had to be one of them. It was a lovely day, and the heart of the world seemed to be soaring with joy. We flew over the skeleton of Cologne Cathedral, and over the Rhine. At the Elbe, Russian fighters met us and escorted us into Berlin. And we flew into a vast cloud of smoke and dust. The great city of five million people was still smoking and burning.

windows.text: And when we came down through this cloud and saw what we saw, we were all speechless. Four out of five buildings in Berlin had been destroyed. That is, completely levelled or completely gutted. This did look like the end of the world. On Templehof Airfield, in that appalling scene of ruin, Marshall Teder took the salute in a march past of 10,000 Russian guards. Then we were driven to Zhukov's headquarters through the rubble and ashes of Berlin. I didn't recognize famous streets I had known well. In that long appalling drive, we saw only 9 or 10 Berliners, all men and women, scare crows, crawling out frightened to see the British and American flags.

windows.text: Just before midnight, we saw the German Phil Marshall Kitel. He was hanged later. We saw him slowly strip off his glove and sign the surrender. Then the Russians gave us a banquet that lasted all night. In the morning we went out again into the fearful scene. The Russians took us to see the smoking ruins of Hitler's famous marble chancellery, but they didn't show us the bunker where he died. As we left, my car was the last in the procession, and at one point, in the clouds of dust, my driver lost the others. We were lost for a few minutes in that utter ruin and silence. In the end of the world, I was afraid. But soon we were in the airplane again, flying out of the ruin, into the bright smile of France and the sunshine of victory and peace.

windows.text: This is Matthew Holtam speaking from London. The last five years Peter Stursberg has covered the United Nations for the CBC, but for his story tonight he goes back to the days when he was one of our war correspondents. It was only the other day that I realized how I came to be the first to broadcast the song of the last war, Lily Marlene. I was reading Dennis Johnston's memoirs, Nine Revers from Jordan, in which he tells how he used to listen to Lily Marlene while waiting for the battle of Alamein. He British Tommy's called it their bedtime song, and sitting on the sand in the sparkling desert night with a wireless tuned in to radio bear grad, Dennis Johnston, who was a BBC correspondent, marvelled at the way a German girl was singing the opposing armies to sleep.

And so it happened that months later I came along and made a recording of the song which somehow got past the censor and was transmitted to Canada via London. I, too, was struck by the way our Canadian soldiers took to Lillie-Marlaine, but what impressed me more was the fact that when we landed in Sicily, the peasants were singing it in the fields. And so, during the lull between the Sicilian and Italian campaigns, I hired a little local orchestra which included an operatic tenor to record Lillie-Marlaine. It's a sad song, really. A sentimental song about a soldier's promise to meet his girl again beside a lamppost in the rain. A promise which is never kept, and even if you didn't understand it, as most of the Canadians didn't, there was its haunting too.

windows.text: Sometime later when we entered Berlin, I was to meet the composer Norbert Schutzer and to learn the full story of Lily Marley. It was a flop, he said, when it first came out. But the war came along, and the gramophone company tried to cash in by changing the name of the record from Lily Marlaine, to the song of the young God. But that didn't work, at least not immediately. The Germans were flushed with victory and via Farrin Gagan England was their song. But the war went on and on, and the Vietnam took radio bear grads and turned it into a forces station for the Africa Corps. Even the Nazis were growing tired when the soldier who was in charge of the new station looked through a pile of old records and saw the song of the young God and thought that it would be a good one to put on at 10 at lights out.

windows.text: Like the time that making a routine political recording of a communist demonstration, I stood with open microphone in the Place de la République and nearby police stopped a small black sedan, from which they jerked a little fat man carrying a briefcase, whose putters went down on my tape. That was Jacques Tooclose, Secretary General of the French Communist Party. His arrest was first an international sensation, and then a subject of comedy. For the police searching his car, found in the back three pigeons, and in the front of radio. The police classified the first as Kerry or Pigeon. Obviously standard equipment for any spy, and the radio is a transmitter. Too close is later able to prove that the pigeons had been both dead and standard birds of eating variety given him by a friend.

windows.text: And the hundreds of mediocrities filled with hatred for the one Frenchman since the war who had tried to govern, screaming their venom, though not one single word of his speech could be heard. And he had to make it to the official stenographer under the reign of cries, Jew, assassin, traitor. I've never considered these anecdotes together before, but I think now looking at them that they contain a common point. The French refused to conform to the norm. All their activities have an element of comedy and tragedy. Whoever has the privilege of covering this country We'll find just watching its people react to history, memorable in itself. windows.text: stories to his native Canada. Just before Christmas, 1941, I was sitting with a friend in a little hotel room in Washington waiting for Wendo Wilkie, a leader of the Republican Party to come back from the White House, President Velt had sent for him secretly. It was a grim time for the democracy. The American Pacific Fleet was sitting at the bottom on Pearl Harbor. The Japs were on the march. Great pressure was building up in the United States to concentrate on the Pacific and let Europe ride. It would have been the popular but fatal decision, and we wondered if Wilkie were getting that pitch. He stumbled into a room. I just can't tell you, fellow. He said he swore me to secrecy. We waited, Wilkie could never keep a secret long. He took a deep breath, then he whispered, Churchill's coming.

Just before Christmas, 1941, I was sitting with a friend in a little hotel room in Washington waiting for Wendo Wilkie, a leader of the Republican Party to come back from the White House, President Velt had sent for him secretly. It was a grim time for the democracy. The American Pacific Fleet was sitting at the bottom on Pearl Harbor. The Japs were on the march. Great pressure was building up in the United States to concentrate on the Pacific and let Europe ride. It would have been the popular but fatal decision, and we wondered if Wilkie were getting that pitch. He stumbled into a room. I just can't tell you, fellow. He said he swore me to secrecy. We waited, Wilkie could never keep a secret long. He took a deep breath, then he whispered, Churchill's coming.

windows.text: Out of flash of conviction, at that moment I felt the tide was turning, I still believe that this was the furthest ebb. To be sure many disasters were ahead, Singapore and Manila would fall, the battleships repulsed in Prince of Wales with the sunk, but Alamine was only six months ahead, and this made Alamine possible. At this Christmas, the grand alliance would be founded, the European struggle, given priority, and Congress and the people reconciled to it. FDR and Churchill had a rendezvous with Destiny. The two leaders gave a press conference, jointly, in the little oval room in the White House where the President worked. FDR sat at his cluttered desk. He had a cigarette in a long holder. Churchill sat beside him thinking bland, with his inevitable cigar.

windows.text: We're prima donners, and I hoped that they were not getting in each other's hair. But obviously the two were getting along famously. As we crowd it in, FDR leaned over to Churchill and said softly nodding toward us, they're an awful bunch of wolves, you know, and I'm going to throw you to them. Churchill chuckled. He answered the first question sitting down. Someone at the back shouted, can't hear you, and someone else said, or see you. When the amazing man stood up, he climbed laboriously onto his chair, beamed at the reporters, waved his cigar, and gave the v-sign. He said simply, here I am. That took care of the press. They forgot the tough questions they'd come to ask. Next day he brought Congress into camp. They shouted with laughter when he commented to a joint session of the House and Senate.

windows.text: He argued with Gering, he interviewed Churchill, Neville Chamberlain, half a score of French primers. He talked to Haile Selassie about the tragedy of Ethiopia. He questioned Maxime Lietvenoff about the Soviet intentions and he wrote and wrote. One of the editors who looked forward to his dispatches was George Rogers. Matthew Houghton was probably the most brilliant foreign correspondent Canada ever produced. To a whole generation of Canadians he was the most eloquent chronicleer of our time, a man who could capture the sweep in the drama of history, and television simple, majestic language, any Canadian could understand. He had come from the small foothills hamlet of Pinscher Creek and Alberta, a solemn, earnest young man of the burning mission to be a great reporter.

windows.text: When he died last week, he could be secure in the knowledge that his country had produced none greater. For 23 years, Halton roamed Europe and Africa and Asia, interviewing potentates and peasants, the great and the near-great, the men of peace and the men of war, and from his typewriter float an enormous stream of words, some four million he estimated recently. He illuminated the great events of an era in a manner so vivid and sharp that all who read him could feel themselves to be eyewitnesses of history. We are proud to have presented in the pages of the Star Weekly much of Matthew Holtman's phenomenal output. In one of his articles two years ago, he wrote, I have not been everywhere and seen everything, but I have been to most countries and seen much.

windows.text: I have been to Carcasson and Karnak and Persepolis and Ceylon and Darjeeling. And I have seen so many of the world's famous places in astonishing and dramatic circumstances. Canadian journalism may never see his lake again. In 1934, Matthew Halton opened a report with a single short sentence. Germany means war, he said. He was a man of peace. And when the war he had foreseen did come, he had to think and write about something that outraged every instinct in him. But it was his job, and he did it magnificently. He saw Finland crushed, and he covered the desert war. And then in 1943, he became Matthew Halton

windows.text: of the CBC, and his voice became a vital link between Canadians at home and their men in Europe. windows.text: and above all the incessant earth-shaking and atrocious pounding of the hundreds of guns. Fortunately for you, the sounds as recorded on a disc are quiet and tame compared to what they are when we hear them. Even bursting shells that shook us and blasted us sound pretty tame on a disc. Listen to this, and then imagine it at least ten times as loud. And now twenty-five minutes have passed. Twenty-five minutes of the most... I think the most ferocious barrage I've ever seen. When I first spoke, we could see the hangars, and we could see the air drum in the first light of dawn.

All we can see is smoke and the terrible burst of flame as one of the huge naval shells on the enemy position. Occasionally an enemy shell falls near us. windows.text: flares going up. But the nervy German, he must be nervy now under barrage of scores, no less than scores of thousands of shells, sending up his flares. So apparently he's still thinking. As I said that I could see tracer bullets from our machine guns going across our front. Now I can hear other machine guns farther away as if from our troops who are advancing. Some of our tanks are going across the front too. So this is the morning. This is the afternoon that was bad enough. This is the morning we waited for. A morning in plan. A morning in in which the fairfields of Normandy are torn and rent and split apart. It was grim, yes, but not all of it, because that was impossible,

windows.text: both for the men who fought and the men who wrote. Don Fairburn is another war correspondent who remembers. It was a quiet moonlight night around the Canadian press camp, which consisted of a few tents in an apple orchard. Action at the front had been limited that day, and a few of us were relaxing over a card game. A game of chance in which the stakes were not high, but confusing because of the variety of money in use. A few intrepid souls had managed to smuggle in some English currency. There were no Canadian dollars, but there were French francs on which nobody could place a positive value in those days and occupation francs, which did have a definite value in our paybooks at least.

windows.text: was the day he could tell us that the nightmare was ended. The German war is over. Five little words that one hardly dares to speak. During long weary years and during hours that seem like years, one sometimes wondered if the carnival of death wasn't a nightmare. And now that the nightmare's over, one has to what it isn't a pleasant dream from which we'll wake, to find the usual mad mornings of war and blood. men have grown old in a morning, gentlemen have grown calloused on slaughter. The best blood of a civilization has been poured away like red wine in an orgy, and then someone tells you it's over, and your first thought is, no more Canadians will die. Not again

windows.text: in this war, will Canadians huddle against the wet earth of Europe in shuddering dons and And then rise to their feet and move out into the storm of steel. Not again shall we see brave men who can stand no more. No more mounds of earth beside the road. The war isn't officially over, but the time has come to be glad. Life can begin again. The anger of the guns has died away. Songs can be heard again. Spring can be spring. day the sun rises as it hasn't risen for nearly six years. And soldiers I've talked to don't quite know what to do about it. They shave and have breakfast. They clean their guns. They try to brush the mud off their clothes.

windows.text: offices. Closely, in as much as our offices, were within a few feet of each other. But like a good artist or craftsman, he was a solitary worker. When he wished to prepare a broadcast, he went into his office and shut the door. Alone with himself, he thought and wrote. He was an efficient worker. He assembled his clippings, his notes, his statistics before him, checked last minute data, and then began to type. Like most efficient workman, he went quietly about his job. He was so quiet about the office that most of the time we didn't know whether he was in or out. He never stormed about to relieve his feelings, or raised his voice in anger, or was rude with members of the office staff. Except on rare occasions in fair verbal fight, I never saw him rude

windows.text: I had a great respect and a liking for Matthew Holton. He was a reporter and a journalist of a competent kind and I am a man of no particular achievement. He was, I think, on the whole more at home in London than even I am. He made much of us, and much of the opportunities that London has to offer. We belonged even to the same club. Only one at a time because I had to leave one, I couldn't bear the back-slaping that met it in mind. If anybody slapped his back, he slapped back, which was more than I could. And now that he is dead, I remember with infinite regret that I was always his guest and never his host.

I remember sitting with him in London one night, and we were talking about the Spanish Civil War, something that was already covered with the fine dust that history uses to dim old passions, and something that a whole generation of young Canadians knows only as a paragraph in a textbook. And yet, more than 15 years afterwards, you could hear the note of anguish in Matt Halten's voice as he recalled those days. He cared about human freedom. He cared about human dignity. And he cared about the simple human right to live a life at peace. And because he cared, he made us care, too. He was a many-sided man, impossible to sum up in a few words. windows.text: And there was a painter on the scaffolding and he was shouting down to his mates on the pavement down below. Absolutely unmentionable curses. His mates were paying no attention to him but looking up to the sky. I was wondering what it was all about. The door of my room opened and in came the floor waiter, who strode over to the window and banged it shut. And turned to me and said, don't you know that windows must be kept shut during air raids? I looked quite blankly at him and he saw that I didn't understand it. He said, don't you know the war has begun? And then it all dawned on me. What the house wanted and what the country wanted was a statement from the Prime Minister. They didn't get it. All he got was a remark from him that we had received no reply from Germany to our communication when they invaded Well, that just about did it. windows.text: As soon as he got to his feet, there was a shout, a fierce, impatient shout from the conservative benches. speak for England, it was Leo Amory, and the whole house chaired, and Arthur Greenwood said, how long were we prepared to vacillate, at a time when Britain and all that Britain stood for was in peril. Virtually he gave Chamberlain 12 hours more to make a statement, neither more nor less, and that was the note on which we went to bed that night. I was on duty outside number 10 Downing Street that morning. It was just like a normal day, except that I had my tin helmet and gas mask. I had no special instructions.

windows.text: Of course there was a bit more coming and going. The crowd began to gather about eight o'clock, but never more than four to five hundred people. Much less than on other special occasions. It was all just the same. The milkman delivered the milk at number 10. Then he went across to the foreign office. As he always did. I remember those last two hours went very slowly. There was constant coming and going. Diplomats, ministers, officials of all kinds. Reporters and cameraman began to gather. It was obvious something big was happening. We knew there was a cabinet meeting. I can remember the church bell rang out for Sunday service, and then they'd been struck.

windows.text: Suddenly, the program was interrupted by an announcer shouting, England declares war. And then they've played God save the king. And this news, of course, was immediately spread all over the city by the loudspeakers in the streets by people who'd been listening in their homes. And at once the streets filled with poles, streaming out of their houses and all of them making with one cry to the British Embassy.
